meta.title: Java Reference
meta.subtitle: Foundations to Expert · Java 25 LTS
meta.renderNote: Canonical source. HTML and PDF are generated FROM this file. Edit here, regenerate both. Never edit the outputs directly.

typeVocabulary.concept: A mechanism or idea — how something works and why.
typeVocabulary.syntax: A concrete form, keyword, or tool invocation.

provenance.verified: Drawn from the learner's actual completed lessons: runtime/toolchain, the Strings cluster, Operators & Conversion, Console I/O, and Dates & Times -- each verified in-session against a real JDK 21/25 compiler, not reconstructed.
provenance.representative: Correct Java, but reconstructed generically — confirm it matches what was actually taught, then I swap in the real breakdown.

topicAreas.name: Language & Runtime
topicAreas.glance: Java source is compiled once into platform-neutral bytecode, then run on any machine by the JVM. Two tools bracket the process: javac compiles, java launches. Knowing which tool does which step — and that the JVM, not the compiler, produces the final machine code — is the foundation everything else sits on.

entries.essence: Java source is compiled to platform-neutral bytecode, which the JVM then executes at run time — so the same compiled program runs on any machine with a JVM.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: javac turns Foo.java into Foo.class (bytecode, not machine code). java starts the JVM, which loads that bytecode and — via JIT compilation and interpretation — turns it into native machine code as the program runs.

detail.label: Why
detail.body: The bytecode layer is the portability seam: compile once, and any operating system with a JVM can run the result. That is the whole 'write once, run anywhere' claim, made concrete.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
javac Hello.java   # source  -> Hello.class (bytecode)
java Hello         # JVM loads bytecode -> runs it

entries.essence: The compiler. It reads a .java source file and produces a .class bytecode file.

detail.label: Form
detail.body: javac takes the full file name, extension included.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
javac Hello.java   # requires the .java extension
# produces: Hello.class

entries.essence: The launcher. It starts the JVM on a compiled class and runs its main method.

detail.label: Form
detail.body: java takes the class NAME only — no .java, no .class extension.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: The final step from bytecode to native machine code happens HERE, inside the JVM — not in javac. javac stops at bytecode.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
java Hello   # bare class name, NO extension
# NOT: java Hello.class  (wrong)

entries.essence: Three nested layers: the JDK builds programs, the JRE runs them, and the JVM inside it actually executes the bytecode.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM. The JDK adds developer tools (javac and friends) on top of the JRE. The JRE bundles the JVM plus the standard libraries. The JVM is the engine that executes bytecode.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: You need the JDK to write and compile Java. A machine that only runs finished Java programs needs just the runtime.

entries.essence: Platform-neutral instructions stored in .class files — the JVM's 'machine code', not the CPU's.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: javac emits bytecode: a compact instruction set the JVM understands. It is not tied to any real processor; the JVM translates it to actual CPU instructions at run time.

detail.label: Why
detail.body: Because bytecode is CPU-independent, one .class file runs unchanged on any JVM — the mechanism behind Java's portability.

entries.essence: The program's entry point — where the JVM begins execution.

detail.label: Form
Mechanism
detail.body: public (callable from outside), static (no object needed to start), void (returns nothing), args holds any command-line arguments.

detail.label: Note
detail.body: Java 25 previews a simplified entry point (instance main methods / simple source files), but the classic signature above is the stable, universal form.

entries.essence: Tells the compiler which package a class name refers to -- required for every class except those in java.lang, which loads automatically.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: Java's standard library is organized into packages (folders of related classes). java.lang (String, Math, etc.) is auto-loaded; every other package's classes must be explicitly imported by their fully-qualified path before use.

detail.label: Form
detail.body: One import per line, above and outside every class declaration -- never inside a class body or method.

detail.label: Example
Common error
detail.body: Placing import inside the class body gives "illegal start of type" -- the compiler is already past the import-reading phase and expects a class member instead. Omitting it entirely for a non-java.lang class gives "cannot find symbol."

topicAreas.name: Types & Variables
topicAreas.glance: Java values are one of two kinds: primitives (raw numbers, characters, booleans, stored by value) or references (objects, addressed by reference). Primitive sizes are fixed and identical on every machine. The distinction between the two kinds is what makes == behave the way it does.

entries.essence: The eight built-in value types, with fixed sizes identical on every machine, stored directly (not as objects).

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: A primitive variable holds the value itself, not a reference to an object elsewhere. Copying a primitive copies the value.

entries.essence: A 32-bit signed whole number — Java's default type for integer values.

detail.label: Range
detail.body: −2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 (−2³¹ to 2³¹−1), stored as 4-byte two's complement.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Counting, indexing, and whole-number math within range. Switch to long if the value might exceed the range.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
int count = 42;
int sum = a + b;

entries.essence: A 64-bit IEEE 754 floating-point number — Java's default type for decimals.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Any calculation with fractional values where tiny rounding error is acceptable — physics, statistics, graphics.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
double g = 9.81;
double avg = total / (double) n;

entries.essence: Name a typed storage slot and optionally give it a value: type name = value;

detail.label: Form
detail.code:
int count = 0;
String name = "Roque";

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: Declares a named, typed storage slot. A primitive variable holds its value directly; a reference variable holds a pointer to an object elsewhere in memory.

entries.essence: Tells the compiler to infer a local variable's type from its initializer, then lock it in exactly as if the type had been written out by hand -- not dynamic typing.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: The compiler resolves the initializer's type at compile time and rewrites the declaration internally as if that type had been typed explicitly. var itself leaves zero trace in the compiled bytecode.

detail.label: Legal scope
detail.body: Only a local variable declaration, inside a method/constructor/block, WITH an initializer on the same line. Illegal for fields, method parameters, and return types -- none of those have an immediate initializer for the compiler to infer from at that exact point.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
var count = 10;      // inferred as int
count = "ten";       // fails -- count IS an int, same as always

detail.label: The Python trap
detail.body: var looks flexible but is not: once inferred, the variable is exactly as statically typed as if you had written int/String/etc. by hand.

entries.essence: A bare whole-number literal defaults to int; a bare decimal literal defaults to double -- and this default is decided before the compiler ever looks at the target variable's type.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: 3000000000 (no suffix) is invalid for a long variable not because long is too small, but because the literal itself is already too big to exist as the int it defaults to. The L or f suffix tells the compiler the literal's type from the very first moment, skipping the default entirely.

detail.label: Note
detail.body: double -> float is narrowing on precision, not range -- 3.14 fits in a float's range but loses precision, so it needs the f suffix or an explicit cast even though the value looks small.

entries.essence: A narrowing conversion is allowed WITHOUT a cast only when three conditions all hold: it's an assignment context, the source is a compile-time constant, and the value fits the target type's range.

detail.label: The three gates (all required)
facts: Assignment context (a declaration or simple =) -- NEVER a method-call argument, even with an in-range literal.

Source is a compile-time constant -- a literal, or a final variable initialized with a constant expression. A plain (non-final) variable never qualifies, regardless of how 'obviously stable' its value looks.
The value is representable in the target type's range.

detail.label: Verified behavior (JDK 21)
rows: final int x = 100; byte b = x;
final int x = 200; byte b = x;
no -- out of range, final doesn't help
no -- method call, not assignment context

detail.label: Why
detail.body: The compiler never traces whether a plain variable's value actually changes before use -- that would require arbitrarily deep program analysis. final is a simple, syntactic, always-checkable substitute: it lets the compiler treat the variable exactly like a literal.

topicAreas.name: Strings & Text
topicAreas.glance: A String is an immutable object — every method that seems to change it actually returns a new one. That single fact explains the literal pool, why == is the classic trap for anyone coming from Python, and why StringBuilder exists. Text blocks add readable multi-line literals, with precise, easy-to-miss whitespace rules.

entries.essence: An immutable sequence of characters (UTF-16) — a reference type, not a primitive, with special literal and + syntax.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: A String is an object holding a fixed character sequence. The language gives it two conveniences no other class gets: double-quote literals ("hi") and the + concatenation operator.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
String s = "hello";
String greeting = s + ", world";

entries.essence: Once a String exists, its contents can never change — every 'modifying' method returns a brand-new String and leaves the original untouched.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: Methods like toUpperCase(), trim(), and replace() do not edit the String in place. They compute and return a new String object; the original is unchanged.

detail.label: Proof
detail.body: Verified empirically in-lesson: calling s.toUpperCase() and then re-checking s showed s was untouched — the uppercased text only exists in the returned value.

detail.label: Why it matters
detail.body: Immutability is what makes the literal pool and thread-safety possible — a shared String can never be altered out from under another user of it.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: Identical String literals share one single object in a cache called the pool — safe only because Strings can never change.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: When the same literal text appears more than once, Java reuses one pooled object rather than making duplicates. Because Strings are immutable, sharing is safe — no user of the shared object can mutate it.

detail.label: Consequence
detail.body: Two identical literals are literally the same object, so == between them happens to return true. This is an implementation detail — never rely on it for comparison.

entries.essence: Explicitly constructs a fresh String object that deliberately does NOT come from the pool — even if an identical literal already exists.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: new String("a") forces a distinct object. So new String("a") == "a" is false (different objects), even though their contents are equal.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Almost never in normal code. Its main value is illustrating the pool: it is the deliberate way to opt out of literal sharing.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: == asks 'are these the same object?' (reference identity); .equals() asks 'do these have the same contents?' — for Strings, always use .equals().

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: == compares references: are both variables pointing at the exact same object? .equals() (as String overrides it) compares the actual character sequences.

detail.label: The Python trap
detail.body: In Python, == compares values. In Java, == on any object compares identity, not value. Carrying the Python habit over is the classic source of String bugs.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: A mutable character buffer — the right tool for building a String piece by piece, because it edits one buffer in place instead of creating a new object per step.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: StringBuilder holds a growable internal buffer (initial capacity 16). append() and insert() modify that one buffer; you call toString() at the end to get the finished String.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Assembling text in a loop, or from many pieces. For a couple of + operations, plain String is fine and clearer.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: Multi-line String literals delimited by triple quotes — readable JSON, HTML, or SQL without escaped newlines.

detail.label: Form
detail.body: Open with """ followed immediately by a newline; write the content on following lines; close with """. The result is an ordinary String.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: Text blocks strip the whitespace you didn't mean and keep the whitespace you did — via a few precise rules worth memorizing.

detail.label: Incidental leading whitespace
detail.body: The common indentation shared by all lines (measured against the least-indented line and the closing """ position) is stripped automatically, so you can indent the block to match your code without that indentation appearing in the string.

detail.label: Trailing whitespace
detail.body: Whitespace at the end of each line is removed automatically.

detail.label: Keep a trailing space: \s
detail.body: End a line with the \s escape to preserve exactly one trailing space that would otherwise be stripped.

detail.label: Join lines: trailing \
detail.body: End a line with a backslash to suppress its newline — the next line continues the same logical line.

topicAreas.name: Operators & Conversion
topicAreas.glance: Operator precedence decides evaluation order; binary numeric promotion decides whether an arithmetic expression computes as whole numbers or decimals. Casting moves a value between differently-sized boxes -- automatic when widening, explicit and truncating (never rounding) when narrowing. The Math class wraps common numeric operations with its own, sometimes surprising, return-type rules.

entries.essence: Multiplication, division, and modulus evaluate before addition and subtraction, left-to-right within the same tier -- not the order the expression is read left-to-right overall.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: * / % share the top arithmetic tier; + - sit below. Same-tier operators evaluate left-to-right. Precedence changes WHICH VALUE results, not whether the code compiles.

entries.essence: int / int truncates any fractional result; % returns the remainder and takes the SAME SIGN as the dividend (left operand) -- a real trap coming from Python, where % takes the divisor's sign.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: 9 / 4 is pure integer arithmetic: truncates to 2, discarding the remainder entirely. 9 % 4 gives that discarded remainder: 1.

detail.label: The Python trap
detail.body: -15 % 4 is -3 in Java (sign follows the dividend, -15), but 1 in Python (sign follows the divisor).

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
9 / 4     // 2  (truncated)
9 % 4     // 1  (remainder)
-15 % 4   // -3 (NOT 1 -- Java differs from Python here)

entries.essence: If either operand of an arithmetic operator is a floating-point type, the other operand is promoted to match, and the WHOLE operation computes in floating-point.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: The / operator itself is neutral -- it isn't 'integer division' or 'decimal division' by nature. It executes whatever arithmetic the operand types dictate. 7 / 2 is pure int arithmetic (both operands int) and truncates to 3. 7.0 / 2 promotes the 2 to 2.0 first, so the whole division is floating-point: 3.5.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
7 / 2      // 3   (both int)
7.0 / 2    // 3.5 (2 promoted to double)

entries.essence: Widening (small box to big box) is automatic and always safe. Narrowing (big box to small box) requires an explicit cast, and for decimal-to-integer casts, truncates toward zero -- it never rounds.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: double y = someInt; needs nothing extra. int x = (int) someDouble; requires the explicit (int) because information could be lost, and Java insists you say so in the code.

detail.label: Truncation direction
detail.body: Casting a decimal to an integer type chops everything after the decimal point, always toward zero -- never toward negative infinity. (int) -9.99 is -9 (closer to zero), NOT -10.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: A utility class of static numeric methods -- each has a specific, sometimes-surprising return type that does not always match intuition.

detail.label: Return types (verified against JDK 21/25)
rows: ALWAYS double, even for whole-number results: pow(2,10) prints 1024.0
Overloaded per type (abs(int)->int, abs(double)->double) -- preserves, doesn't convert
floor(x + 0.5). round(-4.5) is -4, not -5 -- rounds toward positive infinity on ties
Different overload, different return type than the double version
Always double regardless of input type
Mixed int/double args promote the int to double first, same as binary numeric promotion
Range [0.0, 1.0) -- inclusive of 0, exclusive of 1, never exactly 1.0

topicAreas.name: Console I/O
topicAreas.glance: System.out offers three output methods that differ only in line-ending and templating behavior; printf's placeholders match arguments strictly by position, and a type mismatch is always a runtime failure, never a compile error. Scanner reads input by tokenizing on whitespace -- the source of its single most common bug, where a leftover newline from one read silently empties the very next nextLine() call.

entries.essence: print outputs exactly what you give it; println does the same and appends one line terminator; printf builds output from a format-string template with placeholders.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: System.out is a PrintStream instance. print/println differ only in the trailing line terminator. printf takes a format string plus a list of values and substitutes them into %-placeholders.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
System.out.print("Hi");    // no newline after
System.out.println("Hi");  // newline after
System.out.printf("Age: %d%n", 25);  // "Age: 25"

entries.essence: Placeholders are matched to arguments strictly by position, left to right -- and a type mismatch between a placeholder and its argument compiles fine but throws at runtime.

detail.label: Common specifiers
rows: Strict -- a non-integer argument throws IllegalFormatConversionException
Flexible -- calls .toString() on any argument, never fails on type
Defaults to exactly 6 decimal places unless given precision
Platform-correct line separator -- preferred over a literal \n

detail.label: Positional matching
detail.body: The 1st placeholder pairs with the 1st argument after the format string, the 2nd with the 2nd, and so on -- pure left-to-right order, nothing name-based.

detail.label: Type-check timing
detail.body: printf accepts a generic Object... argument list, so the compiler cannot check placeholder/argument type agreement. A mismatch (e.g. %d given a String) compiles cleanly and throws IllegalFormatConversionException only when the line actually executes.

entries.essence: Reads input by breaking it into tokens using a delimiter pattern that defaults to whitespace -- requires import java.util.Scanner, unlike java.lang classes.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: next*/nextInt/nextDouble/next() read a token only up to the next delimiter (whitespace, including the newline from Enter) and leave that delimiter unconsumed in the buffer.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: Any next* method (nextInt, nextDouble, next) leaves the trailing newline unconsumed; if nextLine() is called right after, it immediately grabs that leftover newline and returns an empty string, skipping the real input entirely.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: nextInt() reads only digit characters and stops at the newline -- it never consumes it. The very next thing in the input queue is that leftover newline, so a following nextLine() call finds it immediately and returns "" without ever reaching the real text typed afterward.

detail.label: Fix
detail.body: Insert exactly one throwaway sc.nextLine(); (its return value discarded) right after the next*/nextInt/nextDouble call, before the real nextLine() call that should capture input. Adding a SECOND unnecessary throwaway call consumes actual input instead and can crash with NoSuchElementException once the input runs out.

detail.label: Example
General rule
detail.body: nextLine() called on its own, back-to-back, has no leftover problem -- it is only ever caused by a DIFFERENT next* method running immediately before it.

topicAreas.name: Dates & Times
topicAreas.glance: The java.time types differ by exactly what they carry — a date, a time, both, both plus a zone, or a machine-timeline instant — and the right one is the smallest that holds what the problem needs. Every value is immutable, so arithmetic returns a new object and must be assigned. Amounts split the same way: Duration measures exact elapsed time and Period measures calendar time, and across a daylight-saving change they deliberately give different answers.

entries.essence: Five types that differ only in what they carry; choosing correctly means picking the smallest one that holds what the problem actually needs, because every extra thing carried is another thing that can go wrong.

detail.label: The five types
rows: a zone — so it is not a real moment
a date, a time, and a zone's rules
nothing — a real moment, subject to DST
a point on the machine timeline (UTC)

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: The prefix Local means no zone is attached. A LocalDateTime of 09:05 is a reading any clock anywhere could show; it is not a moment in the world until a zone is attached. Attaching one produces a ZonedDateTime, and that is the precise point at which daylight-saving rules start applying to arithmetic.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: A birthday is a LocalDate -- it does not shift when you fly. A meeting across cities is a ZonedDateTime. A log timestamp is an Instant. Reaching for a bigger type than the problem needs is what imports zone bugs into code that never needed a zone.

detail.label: Note
detail.body: All five live in java.time and all are immutable.

entries.essence: Built through static factory methods -- of(), now(), and parse() -- never with new, because these classes have no public constructors.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: The java.time classes hide their constructors, so new LocalDate() does not compile. Factories are the only door in. Months are 1-based here: 1 is January, unlike the 0-based month of the legacy java.util.Calendar API.

detail.label: The three ways
facts: read the current value from the system clock -- LocalDate.now()

detail.label: Verified behavior (JDK 21)
detail.body: An impossible date throws instead of rolling over: LocalDate.of(2026, 2, 30) raises DateTimeException. It does not silently become March 2 the way a lenient date API would.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: Every plus/minus/with call returns a NEW value and leaves the original untouched, so a call whose result is not assigned does nothing at all.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: A date-time object can never change after it is created. plusDays(1) computes a new object and hands it back; the variable it was called on still holds the old value. This is the identical rule String follows, and for the identical reason -- a value that can be shared safely can only ever hand back a new one.

detail.label: Verified behavior (JDK 21)
detail.body: Month-end arithmetic clamps rather than overflowing: 2026-01-31 plusMonths(1) gives 2026-02-28, the last valid day of the target month, with no exception raised.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
LocalDate d = LocalDate.of(2026, 1, 31);
d.plusDays(1);         // returns 2026-02-01 -- thrown away
System.out.println(d); // 2026-01-31 -- unchanged

d = d.plusDays(1);     // the working form

entries.essence: Duration measures exact elapsed time and is blind to calendars; Period measures calendar time and is blind to exact seconds -- and the choice only reveals itself where the two disagree.

detail.label: The two amounts
rows: calendars -- a day is always exactly 24h to it
exact seconds -- a day is whatever the calendar says

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: A daylight-saving change is the place the two part company. On a spring-forward date the calendar day is only 23 real hours long. Adding one calendar day and adding 24 exact hours therefore land on different clock times. Neither is wrong -- they answer different questions, and the directional consequence follows from which frame is held fixed: Period holds the clock reading fixed and lets elapsed time vary; Duration holds elapsed time fixed and lets the clock reading vary.

detail.label: Verified behavior (JDK 21)
Use when
detail.body: Ask which frame the answer must honour. "Same time tomorrow" is a calendar promise -- Period or plusDays. "24 hours from now" is a stopwatch promise -- Duration. Choosing by unit size alone (days feel big, hours feel small) is what selects the wrong one.

detail.label: Note
detail.body: The ofX factories are static, so chaining them does not accumulate: Period.ofYears(1).ofMonths(2) evaluates to P2M -- only the last call survives. Build multi-field amounts with Period.of(1, 2, 0).

entries.essence: Reports the gap between two dates by removing complete calendar months first, then counting the days left over from wherever that landed -- not by dividing a total day count.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: It advances from the start date by whole calendar months for as long as it can without passing the end date, then counts the remaining days from that landing point. Because months differ in length, the day part depends entirely on where the month-stepping stopped -- which is why the result cannot be reproduced by dividing a total number of days.

detail.label: Verified behavior (JDK 21)
Use when
detail.body: Reporting an age or a gap in human calendar terms. For a plain exact count instead, use ChronoUnit.DAYS.between(), which returns a single total and never splits into months.

detail.label: Note
detail.body: Argument order matters: a start date later than the end date yields a negative Period.

entries.essence: A ZoneId carries a region's daylight-saving rulebook, so zoned arithmetic adjusts the UTC offset automatically and some calendar days are not 24 hours long.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: A zone is a rulebook, not a fixed offset. America/New_York means -05:00 in winter and -04:00 in summer, and the rulebook states when it flips. On the spring-forward date the 2:00 hour does not exist at all, making that calendar day 23 real hours; on the fall-back date one hour occurs twice, making it 25.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Any time a real moment must survive a zone or a DST boundary. A LocalDateTime has no rulebook attached and therefore cannot be affected by any of this -- which is a positive reason to prefer it when zones genuinely do not matter.

detail.label: Note
detail.body: Constructing a time inside the skipped hour does not throw; it is shifted forward to a real time instead.

entries.essence: Viewing one moment from another zone can move the wall clock across midnight, changing the reported calendar date -- the real danger of routing a calendar-only concept through a ZonedDateTime.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: withZoneSameInstant keeps the moment identical and re-reads it on a different region's clock. If that clock has already passed midnight, the same instant reports a different calendar date. Nothing moved in time; only the viewpoint changed.

detail.label: Verified behavior (JDK 21)
Use when
detail.body: Pulling toLocalDate() off a converted ZonedDateTime can hand back a date the user would not recognise as theirs. If the concept is fundamentally a date -- a birthday, a due date -- keep it a LocalDate and never route it through a zone at all.

detail.label: Note
detail.body: Contrast withZoneSameLocal, which keeps the clock reading and changes the moment instead -- the opposite trade.

entries.essence: Formats a date-time into text or parses text back, driven by pattern letters where CASE selects the field and the letter COUNT selects the width or verbosity.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: Upper and lower case are different fields entirely, not styles of one field: MM is month and mm is minute. The number of letters then chooses how that field is rendered. This is why a wrong-case pattern still compiles, still runs, and simply prints the wrong value -- there is no error to catch it.

detail.label: Case decides the field (verified JDK 21, on 2026-01-31T13:05)
Example
detail.code:
DateTimeFormatter f = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("MM/dd/yyyy");
f.format(LocalDate.of(2026, 1, 31));        // 01/31/2026
LocalDate.parse("01/31/2026", f);           // 2026-01-31

DateTimeFormatter.ISO_LOCAL_DATE.format(d); // 2026-01-31

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Reach for a predefined constant such as ISO_LOCAL_DATE when standard ISO text is what is wanted; write ofPattern only for a layout the constants do not already cover.
